---
title: "HW_Power_Analysis"
author: "Elizabeth, Ken, Manav, Nancy"
date: "2026-06-13"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(data.table)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)

library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
```

```{r}
library(data.table)


### VARY THESE INPUTS (based on different scenarios) ###
puzzle_mean <- 3 # average time to solve puzzle in minutes
puzzle_sd <- 5 # standard deviation in puzzle solving time in munites
ATE_Est <- 2 # average treatment effect in minutes
ATE_Variation <- 2 # standard deviation of individual treatment effect in minutes

#### SIMULATE FAKE DATA #######

# Create the potential outcomes table
NROWS <- 300

d <- data.table(
  id = 1:NROWS
)

# Create treatment effect (tau) column
d[ , tau := rnorm(n=.N, mean = ATE_Est, sd = ATE_Variation)]
print(d)



# Generate potential outcomes to control - 'no_label' group
d[ , y_0A_no_label := pmax(0.5, rnorm(n=.N, mean = puzzle_mean, sd = puzzle_sd))]


# Generate potential outcomes to control - 'hard_label' group
### ASSUMED SIMILAR TO 'no_label' GROUP
d[ , noise := rnorm(n=.N,mean = 0, sd= 1)]
d[ , y_0B_hard_label := pmax(0, y_0A_no_label +noise)]

# Generate potential outcomes to treatment - 'easy_label' group
d[ , y_1_easy_label := pmax(0.5, y_0A_no_label +tau)]
d

#Randomly assign treatment
equal_group_size <- nrow(d)/3

d[ , treatment := sample(c(rep('no_label',equal_group_size), rep('hard_label',equal_group_size), rep('easy_label', equal_group_size)))]
d

# Create outcomes vector
d[ , outcome := fifelse(treatment == 'no_label', y_0A_no_label,
                        fifelse(
                          treatment == 'hard_label', y_0B_hard_label, y_1_easy_label)
                        )]

### FINAL TREATMENT/OUTCOMES TABLE ###
d <- d[ , .(treatment, outcome)]
```


```{r}
### RUN 1000 SIMULATIONS TO CAPTURE P VALUE AT DIFF SAMPLE SIZES ####
# Run sample of a specific percentage size multiple times, store in an object, and do this for several difference percentages of sample

samples_list <- list(.1,.2,.3,.4,.5,.6,.7,.8,.9,1)
power_list_anova <- list()
power_list_nolabel_easy <- list()

one_test <- function(d, sample_size) {
  
  n_total <- round(nrow(d)*sample_size)
  n_per_group <- round(n_total/3)
  d_sample <- d[ , .SD[sample(.N, n_per_group)], by = treatment]
  
  d_sample[, treatment := factor(treatment, levels = c('no_label','hard_label', 'easy_label'))]
  
  model <- lm(outcome ~ treatment, data = d_sample)
  
  c(f_test = anova(model)$'Pr(>F)'[1], 
    easylabel_no_label = summary(model)$coefficients['treatmenteasy_label', 'Pr(>|t|)']
  )
  #summary(model)
  #t.test(outcome ~ treatment, data = d_sample)$p.value
}

power_loops <- 1000

for (sample_size in samples_list) {
  p_values <- replicate(power_loops, one_test(d, sample_size)) 
  t_test_rejects_anova <- mean(p_values["f_test",] <= 0.05)
  t_test_rejects_nolabelveasy <- mean(p_values['easylabel_no_label', ] <= 0.05)
  power_list_anova[[as.character(sample_size)]] <- t_test_rejects_anova
  power_list_nolabel_easy[[as.character(sample_size)]] <- t_test_rejects_nolabelveasy
}

power_list_anova 
power_list_nolabel_easy


### PLOT RESULTS ###
power_results <- rbind(
  data.frame(
    sample_size = as.numeric(names(power_list_anova))*nrow(d),
    power = unlist(power_list_anova),
    test = 'ANNOVA F-test'
  ),
   data.frame(
    sample_size = as.numeric(names(power_list_nolabel_easy))*nrow(d),
    power = unlist(power_list_nolabel_easy),
    test = 'Easy vs No Label'
  )
)
power_results

ggplot(power_results, aes(x = sample_size, y = power, color = test)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = sort(unique(power_results$sample_size))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent,
    limits= c(0,1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Sample Size",
    y = "Power",
    color = 'Test',
    title = "Power vs Sample Size"
  )
```


## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
